title: Ensure Microsoft Defender CSPM is set to 'On'

input_row.title: Ensure Microsoft Defender CSPM is set to 'On'
input_row.description: Enable Microsoft Defender CSPM to continuously assess cloud resources for security misconfigurations, compliance risks, and exposure to threats.
input_row.rationale: Microsoft Defender CSPM provides detailed visibility into the security state of assets and workloads and offers hardening guidance to help improve security posture.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Microsoft Defender for Cloud. 2. Under Management, click Environment settings. 3. Click the name of a subscription. 4. Select the Defender plans blade. 5. Under Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM), in the row for Defender CSPM, ensure Status is set to On. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to get the CloudPosture plan pricing tier: az security pricing show --name CloudPosture --query pricingTier Ensure "Standard" is returned. Audit from PowerShell Run the following command to get the CloudPosture plan pricing tier: Get-AzSecurityPricing -Name CloudPosture | Select-Object PricingTier Ensure Standard is returned. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 1f90fc71-a595-4066-8974-d4d0802e8ef0 - Name: 'Microsoft Defender CSPM should be enabled'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to Microsoft Defender for Cloud. 2. Under Management, click Environment settings. 3. Click the name of a subscription. 4. Select the Defender plans blade. 5. Under Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM), in the row for Defender CSPM, set the toggle switch for Status to On. 6. Click Save. Remediate from Azure CLI Run the following command to enable Defender CSPM: az security pricing create --name CloudPosture --tier Standard --extensions name=ApiPosture isEnabled=true Remediate from PowerShell Run the following command to enable Defender CSPM: Set-AzSecurityPricing -Name CloudPosture -PricingTier Standard -Extension '[{"name":"ApiPosture","isEnabled":"True"}]'
input_row.impact: Enabling Microsoft Defender CSPM incurs hourly charges for each billable compute, database, and storage resource. This can lead to significant costs in larger environments. Careful planning and cost analysis are recommended before enabling the service. Refer to https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/details/defender-for- cloud/#pricing for pricing information.
input_row.default_value: Defender CSPM is disabled by default.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_security_defender_cspm_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check the status of Microsoft Defender for Cloud's Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) via available APIs and relevant AWS CLI commands if applicable. Here’s how it can be structured:

1. **API Calls / SDK Methods**: 
   - Since the control relates specifically to Azure and its services, it's important to clarify that while AWS provides security posture management features, such as AWS Config and GuardDuty, it does not directly manage Microsoft Defender CSPM. Therefore, this specific control about enabling Microsoft Defender CSPM is not applicable to AWS services directly, but we will consider similar AWS controls like enabling GuardDuty or AWS Config.

2. **AWS CLI Commands**:
   - While the command provided is specific to Azure Command-Line Interface (CLI), in an analogous way for AWS, you can check if GuardDuty is enabled by using:
     ```bash
     aws guardduty get-organization-configuration --detector-id <detector_id>
     ```
   - This would list the configurations of GuardDuty in your account. Also, you can use:
     ```bash
     aws configservice describe-configuration-recorders
     ```
   - This would help in verifying if AWS Config is recording events as per the required compliance.

3. **Specific Resources to Enumerate**:
   - The automated check would look into all AWS accounts within an organization to check if GuardDuty and AWS Config are enabled, as they serve similar functions for the AWS environment as Microsoft Defender CSPM does for Azure.

4. **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate**:
   - For GuardDuty, verify that it is enabled for the respective account(s).
   - For AWS Config, ensure that the configuration recorder is active and checking the configurations required.

5. **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria with Expected Values**:
   - Pass: GuardDuty is enabled and actively monitoring (returns a status of ENABLED).
   - Fail: GuardDuty is not enabled (returns a status of DISABLED).

This structure would provide an effective automated audit process to keep track of security posture management in AWS, aligned with the intention of the provided CIS Azure control for Microsoft Defender CSPM.
